Name: heterogeneous_quantization
Title: Heterogeneous Quantization for Efficient Deep Learning Inference on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: By applying heterogeneous quantization techniques to deep learning models, we can significantly improve inference efficiency on commodity GPUs without sacrificing accuracy. The key hypothesis is that a combination of hardware-aware quantization and pruning techniques can be used to optimize model performance on specific hardware architectures.
Related Work: Recent works such as AMP-ViT, EMQ, and SNIP have explored the use of mixed-precision quantization and adaptive techniques for efficient deep learning inference. However, these approaches have limitations, such as requiring significant computational resources or relying on heuristic-based methods. Our proposal builds upon these works by exploring the use of heterogeneous quantization techniques that can be applied to a wide range of deep learning models and hardware architectures.
Abstract: Deep learning models are increasingly being deployed on commodity GPUs, but their high computational and memory demands can lead to significant performance bottlenecks. To address this challenge, we propose a heterogeneous quantization approach that combines hardware-aware quantization and pruning techniques to optimize model performance on specific hardware architectures. Our approach involves applying different quantization schemes to different layers of the model, based on their sensitivity to quantization and the computational resources available on the target hardware. We evaluate our approach on a range of deep learning models and hardware architectures, and demonstrate significant improvements in inference efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on a range of deep learning models, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs), and evaluate their performance on different commodity GPUs. We will apply heterogeneous quantization techniques to each model, using a combination of hardware-aware quantization and pruning techniques to optimize performance. We will measure the inference efficiency of each model using metrics such as latency, throughput, and energy consumption, and compare the results to baseline models that use uniform quantization schemes. We will also evaluate the accuracy of each model using metrics such as top-1 and top-5 error rates, and compare the results to baseline models that use full-precision arithmetic.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is that the heterogeneous quantization approach may not be effective for all deep learning models or hardware architectures. Another potential limitation is that the approach may require significant computational resources to optimize the quantization schemes for each model and hardware architecture. To mitigate these risks, we will conduct extensive experiments on a range of models and hardware architectures, and evaluate the effectiveness of our approach using rigorous metrics and benchmarks.
